video_title: The Undervalued Hyperliquid Rival? Is Lighter ($LIT) the Next Hyperliquid?
gist: Bankless host David interviews Flip (Deli Digital) and Will Price (DeFi investor, Lighter adviser) on whether Lighter (LIT), a ZK-L2 perp DEX on Ethereum, is a differentiated challenger to Hyperliquid or merely a copycat. The bull case rests on zero taker fees that attract retail flow and compel market makers to pay higher maker fees, a high-talent engineering team enabling white-glove distribution integrations (Telegram, in-silico), ZK-proven fairness with an Ethereum escape hatch, and a $1.16B FDV versus Hyperliquid's $60B despite buying back tokens at twice the rate relative to supply — all before the US regulated perp market has even opened.

themes.theme: Lighter's zero-taker-fee model vs. Hyperliquid's tiered fee structure

insights.claim: Lighter charges zero fees to takers (retail) and instead charges market makers, while Hyperliquid charges both makers and takers across traditional tiered fee structures, creating a fundamentally different monetization architecture.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: If zero-taker-fee model attracts durable retail flow, Lighter can extract higher maker fees over time — a potential structural margin expansion story as pricing power builds.
insights.quote: Hey takers, we just want you to trade on the platform.

insights.claim: Lighter's zero-fee model reduces the total execution cost for an institutional order-management partner from 5.5 bps (Hyperliquid builder code fee 4.5 bps + in-silico 1 bp) to 1 bp (just the OMS fee), a roughly 80% cost saving that directly drives distribution partner preference.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: Any OMS or aggregator comparing venues on cost will prefer Lighter for routing client flow; this is a quantifiable structural advantage in B2B distribution deals.
insights.quote: the total cost for the user is 5.5 bips versus if you use lighter which has zero fees... the trader you save 4.5 which matters a lot for people who trade volume.

insights.claim: Retail flow on Lighter tends to be smaller market orders rather than limit orders, which is more 'benign' for market makers versus the informed or algorithmic flow (e.g., Jump, Winter Mute) on Hyperliquid, giving Lighter pricing power to charge market makers higher fees over time.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: Benign retail flow is the golden goose of financial exchanges (cf. Robinhood's PFOF model); if Lighter locks in retail dominance, maker fee increases of ~50% are plausible, boosting buyback yields materially.
insights.quote: market makers are so profitable trading... this retail benign flow. My speculation is I think over time you'll see fees increase for makers over time on lighter.

insights.claim: Flip forecasts a plausible 50% increase in Lighter's take rate (maker fees) with no volume growth assumed, which would push the implied buyback yield toward ~20% — a level Flip considers attractive for a challenger exchange.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: A 20% buyback yield at current market cap would be exceptional relative to CEX comps; if fee increases materialize over 1-2 quarters, it becomes a near-term catalyst for token re-rating.
insights.quote: I think there's a world in which you probably see a 50% increase in their take rate... you could have a 50% increase in revenues and therefore buybacks... closer to 20% which I think is quite attractive.

insights.claim: Hyperliquid has no incentive to compress its own fees to zero because it doesn't yet face competitive pressure to do so, but Flip expects fee compression across the perp DEX sector to continue, citing HIP-3 growth mode discounts as evidence already underway.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: Long-run fee compression across perp DEXes is a sector-level dynamic; exchanges with lower structural costs (Lighter's blockchain model) are better positioned to survive a race to zero.
insights.quote: until your hand is forced, like if you're Binance, if you're Hyperlquid, why would you compress your fees when you when you don't need to yet?

themes.theme: ZK-L2 architecture: verifiability, fairness, and MEV elimination

insights.claim: Lighter is built as an application-specific ZK rollup on Ethereum, giving traders permissionless collateral (no trusted bridge or multisig), an escape hatch to Ethereum L1 without team intervention, and mathematically verified matching-engine rules — all key institutional trust factors absent on centralized exchanges.
insights.speaker: Will Price
insights.so_what: These properties are irrelevant until they matter enormously — i.e., a counterparty failure event. For institutional onboarding, the verifiability box-check may be a gating criterion.
insights.quote: lighter has an escape hatch to the Ethereum L1 that can be triggered in the event that lighter has a problem without any intervention from the team.

insights.claim: Lighter's centralized sequencer combined with ZK proofs means transactions are processed FIFO (no MEV from pending transaction visibility), while the sequencer's math is fully auditable on-chain — described as 'you don't have to trust a centralized operator even though right now lighter has a centralized sequencer.'
insights.speaker: Will Price
insights.so_what: Eliminates the MEV tax that bleeds retail on open mempools; combined with the speed bump, this architecture quantifiably reduces adverse selection for takers — a marketing point to quality-conscious retail and institutions.
insights.quote: there isn't any MEV on lighter... lighter has a centralized sequencer and so transactions are processed in the order that in which they're received.

insights.claim: Lighter achieved over 10,000 transactions per second yesterday — by far the highest throughput in the Ethereum ecosystem — while consuming only approximately 1% of blob space, compared to Base's roughly 100 TPS.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: 10,000 TPS at 1% blob usage is a powerful headline for enterprise/brokerage pitches; it signals headroom for massive volume growth without L1 congestion costs, underpinning the infrastructure-provider thesis.
insights.quote: yesterday lighter had something like over 10,000 transactions per second which is far and away the most in the Ethereum ecosystem while only using maybe 1% of the blob space.

insights.claim: Lighter's taker order latency is approximately 20 milliseconds — well under the human perception threshold of ~250 ms — while a mandatory speed bump of 140-300 ms (tiered by staking level) prevents market makers from being 'picked off' by informed/toxic traders, creating a fairer microstructure.
insights.speaker: Will Price
insights.so_what: The speed bump architecture is positively received by market makers (per Will's interviews), which sustains liquidity; it also reduces the slippage experienced by retail traders who would otherwise face cancel-priority abuse.
insights.quote: Lighter's latency for taker orders is somewhere on the order of 20 milliseconds... the market makers don't know that those orders are pending but they can update where they quote in the book as fast as their connection to the API permits.

insights.claim: ZK architecture enables computationally intensive portfolio margining across spot, perps, and options simultaneously — something that Flip argues most competitors cannot do at scale because of the compute requirements, positioning Lighter ahead for on-chain options markets.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: On-chain options with portfolio margining would be a category-defining product; if lighter delivers it before Derive scales, it opens a large new fee stream and attracts sophisticated traders not currently served by on-chain venues.
insights.quote: the choice of using ZK should allow them to do true portfolio margining better than those without, specifically with things with options just because that is very computationally heavy.

themes.theme: Distribution strategy: white-glove integrations and targeting net-new perp users

insights.claim: Lighter's engineering team built a bespoke, semi-custodial Telegram Wallet integration using decentralized rails — a distribution partnership that targets users outside the existing on-chain perp audience, particularly in Southeast Asia where Telegram is the dominant crypto wallet interface.
insights.speaker: Flip / Will Price
insights.so_what: Telegram's user base extends well beyond current DeFi participants; if even a small fraction converts to active perp traders, it meaningfully expands total addressable flow and creates a retail funnel that Hyperliquid cannot replicate without its own bespoke build.
insights.quote: lighter is going after like Telegram wallet... That's like net new users or net new per users.

insights.claim: Lighter has also integrated with in-silico (an OMS/order-execution-management system), and the team's value proposition to OMS partners is eliminating the 4.5 bp builder-code fee that Hyperliquid charges, making Lighter the economically dominant back-end for fee-sensitive institutional flows.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: OMS integrations are sticky — once a platform is embedded in a trading workflow, switching costs are high; winning even a few OMS partners could drive recurring institutional volume at structurally superior margins.

insights.claim: Lighter's engineering team is described as having 'talent density on par with top AI startups,' enabling white-glove custom integrations that competitors with larger user bases but leaner or less capable teams (including Hyperliquid) cannot easily replicate.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: Engineering talent density is a durable moat in financial infrastructure; the ability to build bespoke solutions for partners like IBKR or Charles Schwab could be the key differentiator when those brokerages select an on-chain perp backend.
insights.quote: the engineering team, they have quite a large engineering team. One of the sharpest teams I've met in crypto and the talent is very much so on par with a lot of the startups you see in AI now.

insights.claim: Flip believes Lighter is pursuing a 'super app' trajectory similar to where Hyperliquid is heading — converging exchange, clearing house, custodian, vault/asset manager, stablecoin, borrow, options, and prediction markets onto one platform.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: If perp DEXes converge toward financial super-apps, the valuation gap between Lighter ($1.16B FDV) and Hyperliquid ($60B) could narrow substantially if Lighter executes across product lines; the current gap may be pricing in execution risk, not ceiling.
insights.quote: per Dexes are going to eat all of finance... they're all going to converge into one platform and venue. And I think lighter is one of those that has a decent head start.

themes.theme: US regulated perp market as the untapped prize

insights.claim: No entity — including Lighter, Coinbase, Kraken, or Robinhood — currently holds a US perp license because that regulatory category does not yet exist; the onshore US perp market is therefore still entirely open, in contrast to the already-competitive offshore market.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: The largest capital market in the world has not awarded a single perp license; the first mover to achieve regulatory clarity and execute quickly could capture tens of billions in annual flow — a potentially winner-take-most dynamic.
insights.quote: There's no one with a PERS license inside the United States because that license doesn't exist yet.

insights.claim: Will Price states that Lighter is actively pursuing US regulatory licenses, is US-domiciled (Delaware C-corp), and has the LIT token issued directly out of that entity — a structural advantage over distributed protocols like Hyperliquid that face ambiguous regulatory pathways.
insights.speaker: Will Price
insights.so_what: Legal entity structure and domestic presence are gating factors for obtaining regulatory licenses; Lighter's US corporate structure is a potentially decisive advantage over offshore-native competitors in the race for US perp approvals.
insights.quote: Lighter is based in the US. The lite token is issued directly out of the Delaware CC Corp and you know it there's a presence on the ground with you know all of the important people making decisions on the regulatory side.

insights.claim: The real prize for perp exchanges is integrating as the back-end infrastructure for large US brokerages like IBKR and Charles Schwab — analogous to Coinbase's white-label model for Schwab — with Will Price estimating the commercial terms advantage of a blockchain-based venue lies in structurally lower costs.
insights.speaker: host (David) / Will Price
insights.so_what: Winning even one major brokerage back-end deal (Schwab has ~$9T AUA) could deliver tens of billions in volume to whichever regulated perp venue gets there first; current valuations do not appear to price this optionality.
insights.quote: The real prize are the big brokerages like IBKR or Charles Swab. And winning those as clients can result in tens of billions of dollars of flows in revenue going to whichever per platform gets regulatory approval.

insights.claim: Will Price argues that Coinbase and large financial services firms are unlikely to build high-performance perp matching engines in-house because the talent and complexity cost is high, making B2B infrastructure partnerships with Lighter the path of least resistance — a classic build-vs-buy dynamic favoring Lighter.
insights.speaker: Will Price
insights.so_what: If TradFi brokerages conclude it's cheaper to partner than build, Lighter becomes a B2B infrastructure layer — a business model with high margins, low churn, and valuation multiples more akin to financial middleware than a standalone exchange.
insights.quote: how quickly can I get to market working with someone else as an infrastructure partner versus like how much money am I going to have to spend and how much time is it going to take me to build this in-house.

themes.theme: Real-world assets and long-tail market bootstrapping

insights.claim: Lighter listed the SpaceX pre-IPO perpetual market approximately two weeks before Hyperliquid did, indicating faster listing velocity for novel real-world asset contracts despite Lighter's smaller community presence.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Faster pre-IPO and novel-asset listing velocity is a meaningful edge for capturing speculative trading flow that drives volume and brand awareness; Lighter's ability to launch ahead of the dominant venue suggests internal processes are not a bottleneck.
insights.quote: lighter listed SpaceX preo pre IPO market on Lighter like two weeks before it did on Hyperlid.

insights.claim: RWA perp volume on Hyperliquid has flipped crypto-native perp volume in terms of total volumes — driven partly by speculation around a Trade XYZ airdrop and partly by much lower RWA fees — signaling that the real-world asset perp market is the growth vector for on-chain exchanges.
insights.speaker: Will Price
insights.so_what: RWA perp volume flipping crypto volume on the market leader validates the thesis that asset-class expansion drives exchange growth; any venue that captures early RWA liquidity establishes the reference price and liquidity moat.

insights.claim: Lighter has launched an RFQ (request-for-quote) product layered on top of its on-chain order book specifically to bootstrap liquidity in long-tail RWA and crypto assets, where market maker capital constraints prevent standing limit orders — Flip calls it a 'first-mover' among multi-execution-environment perp DEXes.
insights.speaker: Flip / Will Price
insights.so_what: RFQ dramatically widens the universe of tradeable assets by removing the standing-order capital requirement; Flip's thesis that winners will have order book + RFQ + privacy layer suggests Lighter's RFQ launch is a competitive positioning milestone, and Variational and Ostium are the current RFQ benchmarks to watch.
insights.quote: I wrote a thesis talking about how I thought the winning exchanges would have multiple execution environments... lighter is the first one to have more than one.

insights.claim: Flip's framework for exchange growth is that listing assets people want to trade with leverage that aren't easily accessible elsewhere — e.g., meme stocks, compute, robotics — drives outsized market share gains; Hyperliquid's breakout growth came when it listed assets unavailable on competitors.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: The 'unique listings' playbook is proven; exchanges that identify the next hot asset class and list it first capture viral distribution and sticky traders; Lighter's RWA-focus positions it to capitalize on the next wave of speculative interest in tokenized real-world instruments.
insights.quote: Hyperliquid... really started to gain a ton of traction when they started listing assets that weren't listed elsewhere.

themes.theme: LIT token valuation, buybacks, and cap table quality

insights.claim: At the time of recording, LIT has a circulating market cap of $291M and a fully diluted valuation of $1.16B (a ~4x ratio), while Hyperliquid trades at approximately $60B market cap — a roughly 50x valuation gap despite Lighter's buyback rate being twice Hyperliquid's as a percentage of token supply.
insights.speaker: host (David) / Flip / Will Price
insights.so_what: The 50x valuation gap versus the 2x buyback ratio advantage for Lighter implies either the market is dramatically mispricing Lighter's revenue quality or it is appropriately discounting Lighter's smaller scale and unproven distribution; the investment thesis hinges on which interpretation is correct.
insights.quote: over the last month, lighters bought back, you know, twice the percentage of the supply... as Hyperlid has.

insights.claim: Lighter performs 100% revenue buybacks executed hourly via limit orders placed 10% below market price on its own venue (as maker orders), avoiding self-inflicted liquidity drain — a deliberate mechanism preserving token market microstructure while still accruing value to holders.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: The hourly maker-order buyback mechanic is structurally different from treasury buybacks that hit the market as taker orders; it creates a persistent bid 10% below spot and may reduce downside volatility, making LIT more attractive to longer-horizon holders.
insights.quote: every hour they sweep revenues and then place limit orders down 10% below the current market price and do it as maker orders. So they're not taking liquidity out of their own token.

insights.claim: The LIT cap table includes Founder Fund, Ribbit Capital, and Robinhood as investors — names Flip characterizes as 'generational business' investors seeking 50x returns rather than quick 2x flips, implying a longer unlock/sell horizon than typical crypto venture rounds.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: High-pedigree TradFi and FinTech investors on the cap table signal strategic alignment with the US regulatory and brokerage-distribution thesis; their long-hold posture reduces near-term unlock selling pressure.
insights.quote: The cap table is is very different than most crypto rounds. I mean you founder fund ribbit on Robin like those are very different names than you usually see in a crypto round.

insights.claim: Flip's preferred valuation metric is an 'adjusted market cap' that strips out unvested team/investor tokens and tokens already bought back or staked, then compares to revenues including interest income on stablecoins (noting Lighter is working with Circle and Hyperliquid captures 90% of USDC platform yield).
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: Including stablecoin yield in revenue materially improves the P/R ratio; if Lighter secures a comparable USDC yield-share deal with Circle, it adds a revenue line largely invisible to analysts who only look at trading fee revenue.
insights.quote: Hyperlid gets 90% of USDC that's on the platform. Lighter gets something they haven't announced... they're working with Circle. So let's say it's 50%.

insights.claim: Will Price and Flip both attribute LIT's current undervaluation to: (1) market uncertainty about how to discount revenues at TGE, and (2) poor post-TGE communications that have since materially improved over the last quarter — not to product failure.
insights.speaker: Flip / Will Price
insights.so_what: Valuation gaps driven by communication failures rather than product failures tend to close as awareness increases; if comms improvement translates to institutional coverage and community growth, re-rating could happen faster than fundamental catalysts alone would imply.
insights.quote: I think it was the market not sure how to discount lighter revenues going into their TGE and then I think post TGE it was comms which have gotten significantly better since call it the last quarter.

insights.claim: Lighter currently does roughly 20% of Hyperliquid's crypto perp volume and about 10% of its RWA volume, but is less monetized per dollar traded because it is still in growth mode — Will Price frames this as a deliberate choice rather than a performance shortfall.
insights.speaker: Will Price
insights.so_what: If Lighter is capturing 10-20% of Hyperliquid's volume at a fraction of the monetization rate, the revenue upside from closing that monetization gap (via fee increases) is large relative to the current $291M market cap.
insights.quote: lighter currently does... like a maybe 20% of the crypto volume that Hyperlid does, something like 10% of the RWA volume, but it's not monetized to the same degree. And that's a growth strategy.

themes.theme: Perp DEX sector structure and competitive dynamics

insights.claim: Hyperliquid broke through a $60B market cap and is entering top-10 crypto recognition, with TradFi commentators tweeting about it and spot ETF/direct-access products drawing tens to hundreds of millions in daily flows — signaling the perp DEX sector is being discovered by mainstream finance.
insights.speaker: host (David)
insights.so_what: Mainstream TradFi attention on Hyperliquid raises the visibility of the entire perp sector; a rising tide effect could lift Lighter's awareness as analysts start mapping the competitive landscape beyond the dominant player.
insights.quote: Hyperlid breaks a $60 billion market cap and is positioned to earn a top 10 spot in the crypto industry.

insights.claim: The broader perp DEX sector will have multiple winners analogous to how the CEX market supports Coinbase, Binance, OKX, and Bybit simultaneously — Will Price explicitly rejects the winner-take-all framing for on-chain perps.
insights.speaker: Will Price
insights.so_what: A multi-winner sector thesis means LIT doesn't require Hyperliquid to fail for Lighter to succeed; investors can hold both and position on Lighter's specific distribution thesis without needing to pick a single market winner.
insights.quote: I just happen to think that there are people that enjoy this other spot in trade-off space as well and that the exchange ultimate landscape will have many winners.

insights.claim: Variational and Ostium are identified as the two prominent existing on-chain RFQ brokers ahead of Lighter's RFQ launch — Lighter's RFQ implementation differs architecturally (all trades still routed through the on-chain order book) but provides comparable user fill quality on long-tail assets.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: Lighter's RFQ puts it in direct competition with Variational and Ostium for long-tail asset flow; watch Variational and Ostium volume trends as a leading indicator of whether Lighter's RFQ is gaining traction.
insights.quote: you've got variational osteium the two prominent RFQ onchain brokers if you will and now you've got lighter stepping in.

insights.claim: Flip is watching for lighter's take rate to increase from approximately 0.6 bps (6 bps stated, context suggests this is the current blended maker fee) to as high as 1 bp over 1-2 quarters as a key proof-of-thesis milestone.
insights.speaker: Flip
insights.so_what: The take rate is a directly observable on-chain metric; a move from ~0.6 to 1 bp would validate the pricing power thesis and likely serve as a token price catalyst — making this a concrete near-term monitoring signal.
insights.quote: right now it's like 6 bips. I'd love to see it get up to one bip. If I see that over the next one to two quarters, I'm very excited.
